1: A theme: 'to you, O LORD, I lift my soul.' The total surrender of the self — soul offered upward to YHWH. Christ's Gethsemane prayer is this posture in its purest form: 'Father, if you are willing, remove this cup from me. Nevertheless, not my will, but yours, be done' (Luke 22:42). The soul lifted to YHWH even when what YHWH wills is the cross — this is the acrostic psalm's opening posture fulfilled in the Son's absolute obedience.
2: A theme: 'O my God, in you I trust; let me not be put to shame; let not my enemies exult over me.' Trust and the petition against shame. The resurrection is the answer: the one who trusted was not ultimately put to shame. Romans 10:11 (quoting Isaiah 28:16): 'no one who believes in him will be put to shame.' The anti-shame promise of the gospel flows from the resurrection of the trusting one — he was not put to shame, and those in him share his vindication.
3: A revelation of God: 'indeed, none who wait for you shall be put to shame; they shall be ashamed who are wantonly treacherous.' The universal promise to the waiting ones — 'none who wait for you.' The waiting community (Advent) is those who trust the Father's timing, as Jesus trusted the Father's timing through three days in the tomb. The resurrection demonstrates that waiting on YHWH is not futile: the treacherous who exulted at the cross were put to shame on Easter morning.
4: A theme: 'make me to know your ways, O LORD; teach me your paths.' The prayer for divine guidance — the disciple's request. Jesus is himself 'the way, and the truth, and the life' (John 14:6) — the answer to the prayer of Psalms 25:4 is not an abstract map but a person. The divine path and the divine teacher become one in the incarnation: Christ does not merely show the way, he is the way.
5: A theme: 'lead me in your truth and teach me, for you are the God of my salvation; for you I wait all the day long.' 'The God of my salvation' — the identification of YHWH as Savior. The Hebrew root yasha (salvation) is the root of the name Jesus (Yeshua). Psalms 25:5 prays to 'the God of my salvation' — the NT announces that this God has given salvation a face and a name: Jesus, the God-with-us.
6: A revelation of God: 'remember your mercy, O LORD, and your steadfast love, for they have been from of old.' The appeal to divine memory — YHWH's chesed and mercy as the 'from of old' covenant constants. The incarnation is the most radical expression of this eternal mercy: 'the grace of God has appeared' (Titus 2:11) — what was 'from of old' in divine character became visible and tangible in Jesus. 'From of old' mercy became, in the fullness of time, the mercy that died and rose.
7: A revelation of God: 'remember not the sins of my youth or my transgressions; according to your steadfast love remember me, for the sake of your goodness, O LORD!' The petition for the non-remembrance of sin — what the new covenant explicitly promises: 'I will remember their sin no more' (Jeremiah 31:34; Hebrews 10:17). Christ's atoning work is the basis of this non-remembrance: sins covered by his blood are 'remembered no more' (Hebrews 8:12). Psalms 25:7 petitions what the new covenant delivers.
8: A revelation of God: 'good and upright is the LORD; therefore he instructs sinners in the way.' The divine goodness and uprightness as the reason for teaching sinners. Christ as the divine teacher: 'he opened his mouth and taught them' (Matthew 5:2) — the Sermon on the Mount is the 'good and upright LORD' instructing sinners in the way. The teacher is not a detached pedagogue but the incarnate God who himself is the curriculum.
9: A theme: 'he leads the humble in what is right and teaches the humble his way.' The humble (anavim) as the recipients of divine instruction. 'Blessed are the meek, for they shall inherit the earth' (Matthew 5:5, quoting Psalms 37:11). Christ himself is 'meek and lowly in heart' (Matthew 11:29) — the teacher of the humble is himself the exemplar of humility. He both teaches the humble way and embodies it.
10: A revelation of God: 'all the paths of the LORD are steadfast love and faithfulness, for those who keep his covenant and his testimonies.' Every divine path is characterized by chesed and emet — the two covenant qualities that Psalms 85:10 personifies meeting. In Christ, these qualities are fully embodied: 'full of grace and truth' (John 1:14). The paths of YHWH that are chesed-and-emet lead through the incarnation, the cross, and the resurrection.
11: A direct connection: 'for your name's sake, O LORD, pardon my guilt, for it is great.' The ground of pardon: the divine name, not human merit. Acts 4:12: 'there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved.' The name of YHWH by which Psalms 25:11 seeks pardon becomes, in the NT, the name of Jesus — 'everyone who calls on the name of the Lord shall be saved' (Acts 2:21; Romans 10:13). The prayer for pardon for the name's sake is answered in the name of Jesus.
12: A theme: 'who is the man who fears the LORD? Him will he instruct in the way that he should choose.' The fear of the LORD again (Psalms 111:10: the beginning of wisdom) as the prerequisite for divine instruction. The instruction the God-fearer receives is ultimately Christ himself — the one who is 'the way' (John 14:6). The 'way to choose' is the choice to follow Christ.
13: A theme: 'his soul shall abide in well-being, and his offspring shall inherit the land.' The covenant promise of land-inheritance for the offspring of the one who fears YHWH. Christ is both the heir who inherits (Psalms 2:8; Hebrews 1:2) and the one through whom his 'offspring' (those adopted as children through him: Galatians 3:29; Psalms 4:7) inherit the ultimate land — the new creation (Revelation 21:1-4; Matthew 5:5: 'the meek shall inherit the earth').
14: A revelation of God: 'the friendship of the LORD is for those who fear him, and he makes known to them his covenant.' 'The friendship of the LORD' (sod YHWH, the intimate council/secret of YHWH) — the closest possible covenant relationship. Jesus elevates this beyond servanthood: 'no longer do I call you servants... but I have called you friends, for all that I have heard from my Father I have made known to you' (John 15:15). The covenant secret of Psalms 25:14 becomes the full disclosure of the Father in Christ.
15: A theme: 'my eyes are ever toward the LORD, for he will pluck my feet out of the net.' The constant orientation toward YHWH — the undivided attention that resists the nets of temptation. Jesus's eyes were 'ever toward' the Father throughout his ministry: 'the Son can do nothing of his own accord, but only what he sees the Father doing' (John 5:19). The feet-from-the-net rescue is what Christ accomplishes for those who are trapped: 'if the Son sets you free, you will be free indeed' (John 8:36).
16: A shadow: 'turn to me and be gracious to me, for I am lonely and afflicted.' The loneliness and affliction of the covenant petitioner. Christ entered maximum loneliness: abandoned by his disciples (Mark 14:50), denied by Peter (Mark 14:66-72), forsaken by the Father in the sense of Psalms 22:1. The one who enters that loneliness to its depth becomes the one who promises 'I am with you always, to the end of the age' (Matthew 28:20) — having inhabited every degree of isolation so that no one who trusts him is ultimately alone.
17: A shadow: 'the troubles of my heart are enlarged; bring me out of my distresses.' 'Troubles of my heart are enlarged' — the interior expansion of distress. The Gethsemane prayer is this psalm: 'My soul is very sorrowful, even to death' (Matthew 26:38). The enlargement of heart-trouble to the point of death is what Christ entered so that those who come to him find their troubles can be brought to the one who knows their full depth from personal experience.
18: A shadow: 'consider my affliction and my trouble, and forgive all my sins.' The petition that affliction and sin be addressed together — the physical and the spiritual. Christ's healing ministry addressed both simultaneously: 'which is easier, to say, Your sins are forgiven, or to say, Rise and walk?' (Matthew 9:5). The atonement addresses the root (sin) through which all affliction ultimately flows; the resurrection addresses the consequence (death) that sin produces.
19: A shadow: 'consider how many are my enemies, and with what violent hatred they hate me.' The violent hatred of enemies — what Christ experienced at his arrest, trial, and crucifixion. 'They hated me without a cause' (John 15:25, quoting Psalms 35:19; Psalms 69:4) — the gratuitous hatred of the innocent one. The enemies' violent hatred was the occasion for Christ's non-retaliating love: 'Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do' (Luke 23:34).
20: A shadow: 'oh, guard my soul, and deliver me! Let me not be put to shame, for I take refuge in you.' The soul-guarding petition — Christ's absolute trust in the Father's guarding even through death. John 10:17-18: 'I lay down my life that I may take it up again. No one takes it from me, but I lay it down of my own accord.' The soul that takes refuge in YHWH is the soul that YHWH guards through death and raises on the third day.
21: A theme: 'may integrity and uprightness preserve me, for I wait for you.' Integrity and uprightness as the petition's basis — and as the posture of waiting. Christ's integrity was absolute; his uprightness (righteousness) was the ground on which the Father vindicated him through resurrection. Those who 'wait for YHWH' (Psalms 25:3: 'none who wait for you shall be put to shame') share in the vindication of the upright one who waited through the cross.
22: A theme: 'redeem Israel, O God, out of all his troubles.' The closing petition expands from the individual to the nation — the personal lament opens onto the corporate redemption. Christ is both the redeemer of Israel (Luke 24:21: the disciples hoped 'he was the one to redeem Israel' — and he was, though not as they expected) and the redeemer of all who are 'Israel' by faith (Romans 9:6-8; Galatians 6:16). The redemption from 'all his troubles' is the comprehensive salvation the gospel promises.
